After carefully considering various data source options, we ultimately opted for a data utilization strategy involving the second type, where the data consists of texts provided by the speaker and is subsequently annotated by third-party individuals. This approach is a pragmatic choice, given that we are annotating data from three distinct datasets we have meticulously collected. Using third-party annotators helps mitigate individual bias and subjectivity in the labeling process. Furthermore, this method allows for a more rigorous and collaborative review of the data, ensuring that the labels generated are accurate and reflect the underlying context.\\

The data obtained through this strategy will play a pivotal role in our research, as it forms the foundation for our comprehensive taxonomy, which will be elaborated upon in subsequent sections.\\

\subsection{Curated Data Compilation}

The work relies mainly on three distinct datasets, each providing valuable insights into the research statement. \\

The first dataset [dreaddit], sourced from \citet{turcan-mckeown-2019-dreaddit}, is a comprehensive collection comprising 2837 training and 715 testing records. It encompasses a diverse array of input features, including social media texts, associated labels representing mental health issues, confidence scores, and various other related metrics. The anticipated outcomes span a range of mental health conditions, such as relationships, anxiety, PTSD, assistance, homelessness, almost homelessness, domestic violence, survivors of abuse, stress, and food pantry. Figure [2] shows the counts of the labels in the test dataset.
\ie
The second dataset [pair] \citet{min2022pair} focuses on the brief interactions between the counselors and the clients, offering insights into various levels of reflective listening skills. It consists of 318 records and includes client statements, typically presented to counseling trainees paired with counseling responses that exemplify different levels of reflective quality, spanning low quality, medium quality, and high quality.
\bigbreak
The third dataset [counsel data] \citet{bertagnolli2020counsel} revolves around councelchat.com, an exemplary expert community platform aimed at assisting counselors in building their professional reputation and fostering meaningful connections with potential clients. This dataset comprises 568 records and revolves around therapists' responses to client inquiries, with users having the option to 'like' responses they find most valuable.
\bigbreak
The focus here is primarily on the textual content extracted from these datasets, supplemented by the relevant tags and labels to facilitate a comprehensive examination of the data.

\section{Related Work}
Here we review key publications regarding datasets and the inherent challenges in generative models related to mental health issues
.\\

The problem statement can include various data sources, each offering distinct advantages and challenges. The first type involves individuals providing labels for their text data, offering a direct perspective but introducing potential bias \citep{coppersmith-etal-2014-quantifying}. The second type employs third-party annotators to reduce bias but may result in varying labels due to annotator subjectivity \citep{turcan-mckeown-2019-dreaddit}. The third type leverages medical professionals to assign labels, ensuring the authenticity of tags but presenting challenges in data collection. Lastly, the fourth type utilizes ChatGPT to label text data, benefiting from pre-existing knowledge but acknowledging the limitations of achieving perfect predictive accuracy, even with advanced technology.It may also pose a challenge since it will not undergo human verification, and the annotation will come directly from the machine. The choice of data source entirely depends on the objectives and the research considerations.The paper by \citet{turcan-mckeown-2019-dreaddit} discusses specifically Dreaddit, which is a dataset of 190K posts from five categories of Reddit communities, labeled with 3.5K total segments taken from 3K posts using Amazon Mechanical Turk. It includes ten subreddits in the five domains of abuse, social, anxiety, PTSD, and financial aspects and is annotated by English-speaking Mechanical Turk Workers. Data analysis includes word categories from the Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC) and Yule’s I measure, as well as similar studies on data labeled stressful or non-stressful by a majority of annotators. The study focuses on important details related to measuring the syntactic complexity, which include various menthods and models, both non-neural and neural. The dataset also has the potential to spur the development of sophisticated, interpretable models of psychological stress. Our work in this paper extends prior efforts of Dreaddit by leveraging the "text" column to offer a more comprehensive data resource.he paper \citep{sad_dataset} introduces the Stress Annotated Dataset (SAD), a collection of 6850 SMS-like sentences categorized into nine stressor types, addressing the deficiency in stress management infrastructure. Derived from diverse sources, including literature, chatbot conversations, crowdsourcing, and web scraping, the dataset is accompanied by a proposed daily stressor categorization scheme and detailed efficacy analysis. However, limitations include the prevalence of short sentences, challenges in monitoring labeling processes for potential topic drift, and difficulties in crowdsourcing training data, leading to the inclusion of web-scraped data and potential classification biases. Our work emphasizes sentence length adequacy for effective human annotation, contributing to enhanced data quality.

before_text:
This paper presents a data resource that has been tailored towards the application of enhancing mental health assessments. This dataset includes labeled conversations categorized into stress types, employing a taxonomy validated by a mental health professional. Addressing the limitations of existing conversational agents, we highlight the poor and ambiguous performance of LLMs, emphasizing the need for improved natural language processing tools in mental health applications. To ensure methodological reliability, we incorporated feedback from a domain expert on the development of our taxonomy. Additionally, our resource can be used to develop future tools for medical professionals that can summarize patients' current states through a few targeted questions, offering valuable insights prior to the patient's examination. This research marks a significant stride towards informed mental health assessments, providing a promising avenue for more personalized interventions.

\end{abstract}

before_text:
To enhance the robustness and reliability of our dataset, we conducted a first pass of the annotation process on 59 randomly selected samples. This annotation process was vital in ensuring the accuracy and consistency of the data labels chosen from the taxonomy, particularly in light of the multi-dimensional taxonomy we employed. The involvement of two annotators played a significant role in mitigating potential bias and subjectivity.
\bigbreak
To assess the quality and agreement among the annotators and to measure the subjectivity between them, we monitored inter-annotator metrics like Cohen's Kappa \citet{cohen1960coefficient} and Krippendorff's Alpha scores \citet{krippendorff1970estimating}. Additionally, we observed ambiguity in the results when the tests were conducted using both ChatGPT 3.5 and ChatGPT 4. Figure [3] and figure[4], depicting these metrics respectively, provide valuable insights into the level of agreement and disagreement among the annotators, shedding light on the intricacies of label assignment for future passes of annotation and taxonomy refinement.\\

\section{Experiments}
In our experimental evaluation, we subjected our comprehensive stress dataset to scrutiny within various Large Language Models (LLMs) to assess their performance. This evaluation encompassed popular models such as ChatGPT3.5, ChatGPT4, BERT, and BARD. The subsequent analysis delved into the categorization of responses generated by each LLM, revealing insights into their performance.


The categorization results, detailed in the accompanying table, shed light on the limitations of LLMs. Notably, the table highlights a propensity for bias, with a significant number of responses leaning towards specific categories. This bias raises concerns about the generalizability and reliability of LLMs in capturing the diverse spectrum of stress-related expressions.

Several issues emerged during the LLMs' prediction process. Firstly, the instability of results was observed upon repeated inquiries, indicating a lack of consistency. Additionally, LLMs demonstrated a proclivity to make predictions aligned with the prompt's intended category, possibly indicating a vulnerability to prompt framing. Sensitivity towards mental health nuances, crucial for accurate predictions, seemed to be lacking. Furthermore, there were instances where LLMs exhibited forgetfulness concerning the provided subcategories, further emphasizing the need for improved robustness in handling nuanced queries.

\section{Experiments}
In our experimental evaluation, we subjected our comprehensive stress dataset to scrutiny within various Large Language Models (LLMs) to assess their performance. This evaluation encompassed popular models such as ChatGPT3.5, ChatGPT4, BERT, and BARD. The subsequent analysis delved into the categorization of responses generated by each LLM, revealing insights into their performance.

\bigbreak
The categorization results, detailed in the accompanying table, shed light on the limitations of LLMs. Notably, the table highlights a propensity for bias, with a significant number of responses leaning towards specific categories. This bias raises concerns about the generalizability and reliability of LLMs in capturing the diverse spectrum of stress-related expressions.

\bigbreak
Several issues emerged during the LLMs' prediction process. Firstly, the instability of results was observed upon repeated inquiries, indicating a lack of consistency. Additionally, LLMs demonstrated a proclivity to make predictions aligned with the prompt's intended category, possibly indicating a vulnerability to prompt framing. Sensitivity towards mental health nuances, crucial for accurate predictions, seemed to be lacking. Furthermore, there were instances where LLMs exhibited forgetfulness concerning the provided subcategories, further emphasizing the need for improved robustness in handling nuanced queries.

\textbf{Trauma:} injury, infection, surgery. 
\textit{\textcolor{RedAccent}{I u ent trouh aor a surer I relaed the  int is th a fat at and my mouth is no ired shut for the next  ons.}}\\

\textbf{Physical labor:} over-exertion.   
\textit{\textcolor{RedAccent}{...I don’t mind physical labor... but it’s hard to  run an operation as large as restocking and remerchandising a 200,000 sq ft facility while you are  stuck stocking...}}\\

\textbf{Illness:} viral, bacterial, or fungal agents. 
\textit{\textcolor{RedAccent}{...ended of my work day early today due to feeling sick. I'm assuming it's a stomach virus, as I... ended up hurling everything in my stomach by 2:30 p.m...}}\\

\textbf{Fatigue:} tiredness. 
\textit{\textcolor{RedAccent}{...I have a lot of fatigue anxiety. The more anxious I get, the more fatigued, which makes me anxious...}}\\

\textbf{Hormonal Imbalance:} biochemical imbalances. 
\textit{\textcolor{RedAccent}{I'm a teenager. I get random spurts of anger, like complete, pure rage. I figured it was hormones...}}\\

\textbf{Dietary Stress:} nutritional deficiencies, food allergies and sensitivities, unhealthy eating. 
\textit{\textcolor{RedAccent}{I've not been not eating because I've wanted to lose weight, and I still love awfully unhealthy food...}}\\

\textbf{Dehydration:} when your body doesn't have as much water as it needs.. 
\textit{\textcolor{RedAccent}{...My mood has been worse because it’s so hot in the uk... Maybe dehydration is the reason behind my depressed anxious feeling?...}}\\\midrule

 \multicolumn{1}{c}{\textbf{Psychological Stress}} \\
 
\textbf{Emotional Stress:} resentments, fears, frustration, sadness, anger, grief/bereavement. 
\textit{\textcolor{RedAccent}{No matter how much people say they understand. They don't, they have no goddamn idea what is like there, it was hell and they don't really want to hear it, so I don't tell them.}}\\

\textbf{Cognitive Stress:} information overload, accelerated sense of time, worry, guilt, jealousy, resistance, self-loathing, perfectionism, anxiety, panic attacks, not feeling like yourself or like things are real, and a sense of being out of control.

before_text:
The paper \citep{sad_dataset} introduces the Stress Annotated Dataset (SAD), a collection of 6850 SMS-like sentences categorized into nine stressor types, addressing the deficiency in stress management infrastructure. Derived from diverse sources, including literature, chatbot conversations, crowdsourcing, and web scraping, the dataset is accompanied by a proposed daily stressor categorization scheme and detailed efficacy analysis. However, limitations include the prevalence of short sentences, challenges in monitoring labeling processes for potential topic drift, and difficulties in crowdsourcing training data, leading to the inclusion of web-scraped data and potential classification biases. Our work in this paper emphasizes sentence length adequacy for effective human annotation, contributing to enhanced data quality.\\

While the aforementioned papers concentrate on benchmark datasets, selected works below uniquely delve into the nuances of individual mental health conditions. \citep{sekulić2019just} presents a preliminary study on predicting bipolar disorder from user-generated text on Reddit, utilizing self-reported labels.Feature analysis reveals distinct language patterns, including increased use of first-person pronouns and emotion-related words in users with bipolar disorder.The study explores emotion-expressive textual features, emphasizing the need for early detection systems in mental health. The research addresses the challenge of limited labeled datasets by leveraging Reddit as a valuable source, marking a step forward in understanding and detecting mental health conditions through natural language processing. 
The paper \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1810-09377} investigates the linguistic features associated with schizophrenia through computational linguistics. Analyzing both formal writings and Twitter messages of schizophrenia patients, the study reveals that syntactic features, such as part-of-speech tags and dependency parses, are effective for classification in formal writings. However, a combination of features, including semantics and pragmatics, yields the best results for Twitter data. The Twitter dataset comprises messages from individuals who openly identify as having schizophrenia. This informal and often abbreviated form of communication poses challenges for natural language processing (NLP) tools due to the lack of standardized grammar and syntax.Despite acknowledging challenges, including the informality of Twitter data, the study underscores the potential of computational linguistics for objective symptom monitoring and early detection of schizophrenia.\\

While the aforementioned papers concentrate on benchmark datasets, selected works below uniquely delve into the nuances of individual mental health conditions. \citep{sekulić2019just} presents a preliminary study on predicting bipolar disorder from user-generated text on Reddit, utilizing self-reported labels.Feature analysis reveals distinct language patterns, including increased use of first-person pronouns and emotion-related words in users with bipolar disorder.The study explores emotion-expressive textual features, emphasizing the need for early detection systems in mental health. The research addresses the challenge of limited labeled datasets by leveraging Reddit as a valuable source, marking a step forward in understanding and detecting mental health conditions through natural language processing. 
The paper \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1810-09377} investigates the linguistic features associated with schizophrenia through computational linguistics. Analyzing both formal writings and Twitter messages of schizophrenia patients, the study reveals that syntactic features, such as part-of-speech tags and dependency parses, are effective for classification in formal writings. However, a combination of features, including semantics and pragmatics, yields the best results for Twitter data. The Twitter dataset comprises messages from individuals who openly identify as having schizophrenia. This informal and often abbreviated form of communication poses challenges for natural language processing (NLP) tools due to the lack of standardized grammar and syntax.Despite acknowledging challenges, including the informality of Twitter data, the study underscores the potential of computational linguistics for objective symptom monitoring and early detection of schizophrenia.\\

The authors of \citep{mowery-etal-2015-towards} introduce the significance of detecting mental health issues through social media and propose an initial annotation framework for depressive disorder symptoms. Important points include the utilization of natural language processing techniques to analyze large-scale Twitter data and the creation of an annotated dataset for training machine learning models. Challenges in the study involve addressing the nuances and context-specific expressions of depressive symptoms in online discourse. Our approach differentiates itself from theirs by actively addressing identified limitations. While they acknowledge potential bias in self-reported symptoms and the necessity for refining the annotation scheme to enhance accuracy, we enhance our dataset quality through the addition of supplementary data columns to support annotated datasets.

The existing literature encompasses diverse approaches, contributing to a rich body of work in this field.

Mental health, extending beyond the mere absence of mental disorders as articulated by the World Health Organization on a nuanced continuum\footnote{WHO on Mental Health - {\url{https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/mental-health-strengthening-our-response}}}, is uniquely experienced by individuals with varying levels of difficulty and distress, potentially leading to diverse social and clinical outcomes. Unlike many common health conditions, managing mental health is inherently complex, as treatments effective for one patient may not benefit another. H\\

This paper specifically centers its focus on:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Comprehensive Dataset and Taxonomy: We craft a detailed dataset with a nuanced taxonomy, moving beyond conventional stress detection to precise categorization, enriching our understanding of mental health challenges.
    \item Bridging the Gap and Resource for Researchers: Our work connects existing dataset labels to our taxonomy, advancing mental health support technology. It serves as a valuable resource, addressing gaps in categorization for researchers.
    \item Holistic Support and Efficient Healthcare: Our goal is direct support for individuals and  medical professionals, streamlining healthcare and reducing patient burden. This aligns with our expectations of enhancing holistic well-being and healthcare efficiency through our dataset.
\end{itemize}

% The goal is to provide direct support to individuals, offering reassurance, companionship, and an understanding of their experiences. Beyond assisting individuals, the aim is to equip medical professionals with preexisting knowledge about their patients derived from the summarized responses gathered prior to their scheduled interactions. This information can aid healthcare professionals in promptly grasping the patients' conditions and assisting without requiring patients to recount their uncomfortable experiences repeatedly.\\

In pursuit of our objective, we have pioneered an innovative approach to meticulously curate a comprehensive and detailed dataset, complemented by a carefully constructed taxonomy. Unlike conventional models that merely identify stress without context, our methodology involves categorizing stress into specific and nuanced categories, enriching our understanding of mental health challenges. Importantly, the validity of our taxonomy is bolstered by evaluation from adult therapist, ensuring its relevance and accuracy in clinical contexts. Constructing such a taxonomy poses significant challenges, given the nuanced nature of mental health; however, our motivation lies in the potential for a more precise and personalized approach to support. We hope that our annotated dataset will provide valuable insights into various types of stress, serving as a resource to train models that can effectively and efficiently engage with both patients and healthcare professionals. Furthermore, this paper serves as a unique and invaluable resource for researchers, addressing the incomplete categorization in existing datasets and bridging the gap between their labels and our systematically constructed taxonomy, thereby contributing to the advancement of mental health support technology with a more comprehensive and nuanced solution.

\section{Related Work}
Here we review key publications regarding datasets and the inherent challenges in generative models related to mental health issues.\\

The problem statement can include various data sources, each offering distinct advantages and challenges. The first type involves individuals providing labels for their text data, offering a direct perspective but introducing potential bias \citep{coppersmith-etal-2014-quantifying}. The second type employs third-party annotators to reduce bias but may result in varying labels due to annotator subjectivity \citep{turcan-mckeown-2019-dreaddit}. The third type leverages medical professionals to assign labels, ensuring the authenticity of tags but presenting challenges in data collection \citet{bertagnolli2020counsel}. After exploring various data sources, we theoretically defined a fourth type which utilizes large language models (LLMs) to label text data, benefiting from pre-existing knowledge but acknowledging the limitations of achieving perfect predictive accuracy, even with advanced technology.It may also pose a challenge since it will not undergo human verification, and the annotation will come directly from the machine. The choice of data source entirely depends on the objectives and the research considerations.\\

% The paper by \citet{turcan-mckeown-2019-dreaddit} discusses specifically Dreaddit, which is a dataset of 190K posts from five categories of Reddit communities, labeled with 3.5K total segments taken from 3K posts using Amazon Mechanical Turk. It includes ten subreddits in the five domains of abuse, social, anxiety, PTSD, and financial aspects and is annotated by English-speaking Mechanical Turk Workers. Data analysis includes word categories from the Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC) and Yule’s I measure, as well as similar studies on data labeled stressful or non-stressful by a majority of annotators. The study focuses on important details related to measuring the syntactic complexity, which include various methods and models, both non-neural and neural. Our work in this paper extends prior efforts of Dreaddit by leveraging the "text" column to offer a more comprehensive data resource.Whereas 
The paper \citep{sad_dataset} introduces the Stress Annotated Dataset (SAD), a collection of 6850 SMS-like sentences categorized into nine stressor types, addressing the deficiency in stress management infrastructure. Derived from diverse sources, including literature, chatbot conversations, crowdsourcing, and web scraping, the dataset is accompanied by a proposed daily stressor categorization scheme and detailed efficacy analysis. However, limitations include the prevalence of short sentences, challenges in monitoring labeling processes for potential topic drift, and difficulties in crowdsourcing training data, leading to the inclusion of web-scraped data and potential classification biases. Our work in this paper emphasizes sentence length adequacy for effective human annotation, contributing to enhanced data quality.\\

\bigbreak
The categorization results, detailed in able , shed light on the limitations of LLMs. Notably, the table highlights a propensity for bias, with a significant number of responses leaning towards specific categories. This bias raises concerns about the generalizability and reliability of LLMs in capturing the diverse spectrum of stress-related expressions.

\bigbreak
Several issues emerged during the LLMs' prediction process. Firstly, the instability of results was observed upon repeated inquiries, indicating a lack of consistency. Sensitivity towards mental health nuances, crucial for accurate predictions, seemed to be lacking. before_text:
\section{Experiments}
In our experimental evaluation, we subjected our non-syntactic dataset (Stressmix) to scrutiny within various Large Language Models (LLMs) to assess their performance. This evaluation encompassed popular models such as ChatGPT3.5, ChatGPT4, and BARD. The subsequent analysis delved into the categorization of responses generated by each LLM, revealing insights into their performance. \\

The prompts supplied to the LLMs included an example from each category and its corresponding subcategory, aiding the model in understanding these classifications. An illustrative query example is available for your reference.

\bigbreak
Sample prompt:
\bigbreak

\textit{Could you kindly assist me in classifying the text below into a single label from the provided type and subtype, accompanied by examples? Please ensure your response includes both the category and the subcategory. Refer to the examples below for guidance.}
\bigbreak
\textit{
Type: Psychosocial\\
Sub-type: Lack of social support(having no friends or relatives whom they can count on in times of trouble.)\\
Example:I have no real friends. I have a girlfriend who irritates me but loves me to death. I push her away and pushes me away. We‚I're going through a breakup, and I have nobody.
...\\
\\
Text:
We had great chemistry, but then he became distant. I had the feeling that I can't be without him. As soon as I felt the difference, I was scared to lose him. I started freaking out if he did not answer, thinking that something bad happened.
}

\bigbreak

Similar to Psychosocial - Lack of social support, an individual example was provided for each category and its respective subcategory, accompanied by the associated query text.


\bigbreak
The categorization results, detailed in Table \ref{tab:predicted_distribution}, shed light on the limitations of LLMs. Notably, the table highlights a propensity for bias, with a significant number of responses leaning towards specific categories. This bias raises concerns about the generalizability and reliability of LLMs in capturing the diverse spectrum of stress-related expressions.

\bigbreak
Several issues emerged during the LLMs' prediction process. Firstly, the instability of results was observed upon repeated inquiries, indicating a lack of consistency. Additionally, LLMs demonstrated a proclivity to make predictions aligned with the prompt's intended category, possibly indicating a vulnerability to prompt framing. Sensitivity towards mental health nuances, crucial for accurate predictions, seemed to be lacking. Furthermore, there were instances where LLMs exhibited forgetfulness concerning the provided subcategories, further emphasizing the need for improved robustness in handling nuanced queries.

After carefully considering various data source options, we ultimately opted for a data utilization strategy involving the second type, where the data consists of texts provided by the speaker and is subsequently annotated by third-party individuals. This approach is a pragmatic choice, given that we are annotating data from three distinct datasets we have meticulously collected. Using third-party annotators helps mitigate individual bias and subjectivity in the labeling process. Furthermore, this method allows for a more rigorous and collaborative review of the data, ensuring that the labels generated are accurate and reflect the underlying context.\\

The data obtained through this strategy will play a pivotal role in our research, as it forms the foundation for our comprehensive taxonomy, which will be elaborated upon in subsequent sections.\\

\subsection{Curated Data Compilation}

The work relies mainly on three distinct datasets, each providing valuable insights into the research statement. \\

The first dataset [dreaddit], sourced from \citet{turcan-mckeown-2019-dreaddit}, is a comprehensive collection comprising 2837 training and 715 testing records. It encompasses a diverse array of input features, including social media texts, associated labels representing mental health issues, confidence scores, and various other related metrics. The anticipated outcomes span a range of mental health conditions, such as relationships, anxiety, PTSD, assistance, homelessness, almost homelessness, domestic violence, survivors of abuse, stress, and food pantry. Figure [2] shows the counts of the labels in the test dataset.
\ie
The second dataset [pair] \citet{min2022pair} focuses on the brief interactions between the counselors and the clients, offering insights into various levels of reflective listening skills. It consists of 318 records and includes client statements, typically presented to counseling trainees paired with counseling responses that exemplify different levels of reflective quality, spanning low quality, medium quality, and high quality.
\bigbreak
The third dataset [counsel data] \citet{bertagnolli2020counsel} revolves around councelchat.com, an exemplary expert community platform aimed at assisting counselors in building their professional reputation and fostering meaningful connections with potential clients. This dataset comprises 568 records and revolves around therapists' responses to client inquiries, with users having the option to 'like' responses they find most valuable.
\bigbreak
The focus here is primarily on the textual content extracted from these datasets, supplemented by the relevant tags and labels to facilitate a comprehensive examination of the data.

\section{Related Work}
Here we review key publications regarding datasets and the inherent challenges in generative models related to mental health issues
.\\

The problem statement can include various data sources, each offering distinct advantages and challenges. The first type involves individuals providing labels for their text data, offering a direct perspective but introducing potential bias \citep{coppersmith-etal-2014-quantifying}. The second type employs third-party annotators to reduce bias but may result in varying labels due to annotator subjectivity \citep{turcan-mckeown-2019-dreaddit}. The third type leverages medical professionals to assign labels, ensuring the authenticity of tags but presenting challenges in data collection. Lastly, the fourth type utilizes ChatGPT to label text data, benefiting from pre-existing knowledge but acknowledging the limitations of achieving perfect predictive accuracy, even with advanced technology.It may also pose a challenge since it will not undergo human verification, and the annotation will come directly from the machine. The choice of data source entirely depends on the objectives and the research considerations.The paper by \citet{turcan-mckeown-2019-dreaddit} discusses specifically Dreaddit, which is a dataset of 190K posts from five categories of Reddit communities, labeled with 3.5K total segments taken from 3K posts using Amazon Mechanical Turk. It includes ten subreddits in the five domains of abuse, social, anxiety, PTSD, and financial aspects and is annotated by English-speaking Mechanical Turk Workers. Data analysis includes word categories from the Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC) and Yule’s I measure, as well as similar studies on data labeled stressful or non-stressful by a majority of annotators. The study focuses on important details related to measuring the syntactic complexity, which include various menthods and models, both non-neural and neural. The dataset also has the potential to spur the development of sophisticated, interpretable models of psychological stress. Our work in this paper extends prior efforts of Dreaddit by leveraging the "text" column to offer a more comprehensive data resource.he paper \citep{sad_dataset} introduces the Stress Annotated Dataset (SAD), a collection of 6850 SMS-like sentences categorized into nine stressor types, addressing the deficiency in stress management infrastructure. Derived from diverse sources, including literature, chatbot conversations, crowdsourcing, and web scraping, the dataset is accompanied by a proposed daily stressor categorization scheme and detailed efficacy analysis. However, limitations include the prevalence of short sentences, challenges in monitoring labeling processes for potential topic drift, and difficulties in crowdsourcing training data, leading to the inclusion of web-scraped data and potential classification biases. Our work emphasizes sentence length adequacy for effective human annotation, contributing to enhanced data quality.

before_text:
This paper presents a data resource that has been tailored towards the application of enhancing mental health assessments. This dataset includes labeled conversations categorized into stress types, employing a taxonomy validated by a mental health professional. Addressing the limitations of existing conversational agents, we highlight the poor and ambiguous performance of LLMs, emphasizing the need for improved natural language processing tools in mental health applications. To ensure methodological reliability, we incorporated feedback from a domain expert on the development of our taxonomy. Additionally, our resource can be used to develop future tools for medical professionals that can summarize patients' current states through a few targeted questions, offering valuable insights prior to the patient's examination. This research marks a significant stride towards informed mental health assessments, providing a promising avenue for more personalized interventions.

\end{abstract}

before_text:
To enhance the robustness and reliability of our dataset, we conducted a first pass of the annotation process on 59 randomly selected samples. This annotation process was vital in ensuring the accuracy and consistency of the data labels chosen from the taxonomy, particularly in light of the multi-dimensional taxonomy we employed. The involvement of two annotators played a significant role in mitigating potential bias and subjectivity.
\bigbreak
To assess the quality and agreement among the annotators and to measure the subjectivity between them, we monitored inter-annotator metrics like Cohen's Kappa \citet{cohen1960coefficient} and Krippendorff's Alpha scores \citet{krippendorff1970estimating}. Additionally, we observed ambiguity in the results when the tests were conducted using both ChatGPT 3.5 and ChatGPT 4. Figure [3] and figure[4], depicting these metrics respectively, provide valuable insights into the level of agreement and disagreement among the annotators, shedding light on the intricacies of label assignment for future passes of annotation and taxonomy refinement.\\

\section{Experiments}
In our experimental evaluation, we subjected our comprehensive stress dataset to scrutiny within various Large Language Models (LLMs) to assess their performance. This evaluation encompassed popular models such as ChatGPT3.5, ChatGPT4, BERT, and BARD. The subsequent analysis delved into the categorization of responses generated by each LLM, revealing insights into their performance.


The categorization results, detailed in the accompanying table, shed light on the limitations of LLMs. Notably, the table highlights a propensity for bias, with a significant number of responses leaning towards specific categories. This bias raises concerns about the generalizability and reliability of LLMs in capturing the diverse spectrum of stress-related expressions.

Several issues emerged during the LLMs' prediction process. Firstly, the instability of results was observed upon repeated inquiries, indicating a lack of consistency. Additionally, LLMs demonstrated a proclivity to make predictions aligned with the prompt's intended category, possibly indicating a vulnerability to prompt framing. Sensitivity towards mental health nuances, crucial for accurate predictions, seemed to be lacking. Furthermore, there were instances where LLMs exhibited forgetfulness concerning the provided subcategories, further emphasizing the need for improved robustness in handling nuanced queries.

before_text:
The paper \citep{sad_dataset} introduces the Stress Annotated Dataset (SAD), a collection of 6850 SMS-like sentences categorized into nine stressor types, addressing the deficiency in stress management infrastructure. Derived from diverse sources, including literature, chatbot conversations, crowdsourcing, and web scraping, the dataset is accompanied by a proposed daily stressor categorization scheme and detailed efficacy analysis. However, limitations include the prevalence of short sentences, challenges in monitoring labeling processes for potential topic drift, and difficulties in crowdsourcing training data, leading to the inclusion of web-scraped data and potential classification biases. Our work in this paper emphasizes sentence length adequacy for effective human annotation, contributing to enhanced data quality.\\

While the aforementioned papers concentrate on benchmark datasets, selected works below uniquely delve into the nuances of individual mental health conditions. \citep{sekulić2019just} presents a preliminary study on predicting bipolar disorder from user-generated text on Reddit, utilizing self-reported labels.Feature analysis reveals distinct language patterns, including increased use of first-person pronouns and emotion-related words in users with bipolar disorder.The study explores emotion-expressive textual features, emphasizing the need for early detection systems in mental health. The research addresses the challenge of limited labeled datasets by leveraging Reddit as a valuable source, marking a step forward in understanding and detecting mental health conditions through natural language processing. 
The paper \citep{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1810-09377} investigates the linguistic features associated with schizophrenia through computational linguistics. Analyzing both formal writings and Twitter messages of schizophrenia patients, the study reveals that syntactic features, such as part-of-speech tags and dependency parses, are effective for classification in formal writings. However, a combination of features, including semantics and pragmatics, yields the best results for Twitter data. The Twitter dataset comprises messages from individuals who openly identify as having schizophrenia. This informal and often abbreviated form of communication poses challenges for natural language processing (NLP) tools due to the lack of standardized grammar and syntax.Despite acknowledging challenges, including the informality of Twitter data, the study underscores the potential of computational linguistics for objective symptom monitoring and early detection of schizophrenia.\\

before_text:
Mental health, extending beyond the mere absence of mental disorders as articulated by the World Health Organization on a nuanced continuum\footnote{WHO on Mental Health - {\url{https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/mental-health-strengthening-our-response}}}, is uniquely experienced by individuals with varying levels of difficulty and distress, potentially leading to diverse social and clinical outcomes. Unlike many common health conditions, managing mental health is inherently complex, as treatments effective for one patient may not benefit another. H\\

This paper specifically centers its focus on:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Comprehensive Dataset and Taxonomy: We craft a detailed dataset with a nuanced taxonomy, moving beyond conventional stress detection to precise categorization, enriching our understanding of mental health challenges.
    \item Bridging the Gap and Resource for Researchers: Our work connects existing dataset labels to our taxonomy, advancing mental health support technology. It serves as a valuable resource, addressing gaps in categorization for researchers.
    \item Holistic Support and Efficient Healthcare: Our goal is direct support for individuals and  medical professionals, streamlining healthcare and reducing patient burden. This aligns with our expectations of enhancing holistic well-being and healthcare efficiency through our dataset.
\end{itemize}

% The goal is to provide direct support to individuals, offering reassurance, companionship, and an understanding of their experiences. Beyond assisting individuals, the aim is to equip medical professionals with preexisting knowledge about their patients derived from the summarized responses gathered prior to their scheduled interactions. This information can aid healthcare professionals in promptly grasping the patients' conditions and assisting without requiring patients to recount their uncomfortable experiences repeatedly.\\

In pursuit of our objective, we have pioneered an innovative approach to meticulously curate a comprehensive and detailed dataset, complemented by a carefully constructed taxonomy. Unlike conventional models that merely identify stress without context, our methodology involves categorizing stress into specific and nuanced categories, enriching our understanding of mental health challenges. Importantly, the validity of our taxonomy is bolstered by evaluation from adult therapist, ensuring its relevance and accuracy in clinical contexts. Constructing such a taxonomy poses significant challenges, given the nuanced nature of mental health; however, our motivation lies in the potential for a more precise and personalized approach to support. We hope that our annotated dataset will provide valuable insights into various types of stress, serving as a resource to train models that can effectively and efficiently engage with both patients and healthcare professionals. Furthermore, this paper serves as a unique and invaluable resource for researchers, addressing the incomplete categorization in existing datasets and bridging the gap between their labels and our systematically constructed taxonomy, thereby contributing to the advancement of mental health support technology with a more comprehensive and nuanced solution.

\section{Related Work}
Here we review key publications regarding datasets and the inherent challenges in generative models related to mental health issues.\\

The problem statement can include various data sources, each offering distinct advantages and challenges. The first type involves individuals providing labels for their text data, offering a direct perspective but introducing potential bias \citep{coppersmith-etal-2014-quantifying}. The second type employs third-party annotators to reduce bias but may result in varying labels due to annotator subjectivity \citep{turcan-mckeown-2019-dreaddit}. The third type leverages medical professionals to assign labels, ensuring the authenticity of tags but presenting challenges in data collection \citet{bertagnolli2020counsel}. After exploring various data sources, we theoretically defined a fourth type which utilizes large language models (LLMs) to label text data, benefiting from pre-existing knowledge but acknowledging the limitations of achieving perfect predictive accuracy, even with advanced technology.It may also pose a challenge since it will not undergo human verification, and the annotation will come directly from the machine. The choice of data source entirely depends on the objectives and the research considerations.\\

% The paper by \citet{turcan-mckeown-2019-dreaddit} discusses specifically Dreaddit, which is a dataset of 190K posts from five categories of Reddit communities, labeled with 3.5K total segments taken from 3K posts using Amazon Mechanical Turk. It includes ten subreddits in the five domains of abuse, social, anxiety, PTSD, and financial aspects and is annotated by English-speaking Mechanical Turk Workers. Data analysis includes word categories from the Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC) and Yule’s I measure, as well as similar studies on data labeled stressful or non-stressful by a majority of annotators. The study focuses on important details related to measuring the syntactic complexity, which include various methods and models, both non-neural and neural. Our work in this paper extends prior efforts of Dreaddit by leveraging the "text" column to offer a more comprehensive data resource.Whereas

Our work in this paper emphasizes sentence length adequacy for effective human annotation, contributing to enhanced data quality.\\

Several issues emerged during the LLMs' prediction process. Firstly, the instability of results was observed upon repeated inquiries, indicating a lack of consistency. Additionally, LLMs demonstrated a proclivity to make predictions aligned with the prompt's intended category, possibly indicating a vulnerability to prompt framing. Sensitivity towards mental health nuances, crucial for accurate predictions, seemed to be lacking. Furthermore, there were instances where LLMs exhibited forgetfulness concerning the provided subcategories, further emphasizing the need for improved robustness in handling nuanced queries.

\section{Related Work}\label{sec:related_work}



In the wake of recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs), the imperative to discern machine-generated texts has grown significantly, accompanied by formidable challenges. Various existing methodologies encompass fine-tuning Language Models (LMs) with supervised datasets, potentially utilizing techniques like contrastive learning, adversarial learning, or human-assisted learning. Additionally, there are zero-shot methods that leverage statistical features of texts or intermediate values within LLMs, watermarking techniques that introduce "green tokens" or embed "secret keys" as vectors to predicted outputs, and the innovative use of (another) LLM as detectors through prompting. A comprehensive overview of these approaches can be found in \citet{wu2023survey}.

While recent studies in the detection domain predominantly focus on surface-level features like n-gram distributions and part-of-speech (POS) tags, our approach deviates by emphasizing hierarchical discourse features within texts. Rather than solely relying on traditional indicators, we seek to address the challenge from a different perspective. This shift is reminiscent of the work by \citet{corston-oliver-etal-2001-machine}, who, in the pre-LLM era, explored a comparable path. Their analysis delved into the utility of branching properties in POS trees to ascertain whether texts were machine-generated, specifically by a machine translation model. This unique angle offers a novel strategy to tackle the complexities associated with identifying machine-generated content.

\section{Related Work}\label{sec:related_work}
With the advent of recent LLMs, the need as well as the challenges in distinguishing machine-generated texts has risen significantly. Various existing methodologies encompass finetuning LMs with supervised datasets, potentially utilizing techniques like contrastive learning, adversarial learning, or human-assisted learning. Additionally, there are zero-shot methods that leverage statistical features of texts or intermediate values within LLMs, watermarking techniques that introduce ``green tokens'' or embed ``secret keys'' as vectors to predicted outputs, and prompt-based approaches that make use of (another) LLM as detectors. A comprehensive overview of these approaches can be found in \citet{wu2023survey}.

While the relevant recent studies on the detection task primarily adopt surface-level features, such as distributions of n-grams and part-of-speech (POS) tags, we approach the problem from another angle, namely using hierarchical discourse features within texts. This direction is reminiscent of the work by \citet{corston-oliver-etal-2001-machine}, who, in the pre-LLM era, explored a comparable path. Their study delved into the utility of branching properties of POS trees to ascertain whether texts were machine-generated, specifically by a machine translation model. In our study, we consider discourse-level features that span over multiple sentences and paragraphs (as opposed to sentence-level POS features).




The existing challenges within the domain encompass several facets. Firstly, there are pronounced out-of-distribution (OOD) challenges, wherein the detectors encounter difficulties when confronted with data that falls outside the learned distribution. This is particularly evident in the case of Large Language Models (LLMs). Secondly, the field grapples with potential attacks, such as paraphrasing attacks, where machine-generated texts undergo further paraphrasing to alter the distribution of lexical and syntactic features. This dynamic renders detectors reliant on surface-level features and watermarking technology ineffective in the face of sophisticated manipulation. Lastly, and perhaps most significantly, there is a notable escalation in the inherent ambiguities present in texts, making it progressively challenging to distinguish between those generated by humans and machines.





Current challenges in the field include (1) out-of-distribution (OOD) challenges where the learned detectors struggle on OOD data and LLMs, (2) potential attacks such as paraphrasing attacks where machine-generated texts are further paraphrased to change the distribution of lexical and syntactic features. This renders detectors based on surface-level features and watermarking technology ineffective. (3) Last but not least, the inherent ambiguities in the texts between human- and machine-generated ones are increasing.

\section{Related Work}\label{sec:related_work}
With the advent of recent LLMs, the need as well as the challenges in distinguishing machine-generated texts has risen significantly. Various existing methodologies encompass finetuning LMs \citep{guo2023close,liu2023argugpt,liu2023check,li2023deepfake,} with supervised datasets, potentially utilizing techniques like contrastive learning, adversarial learning, or human-assisted learning. Additionally, there are zero-shot methods that leverage statistical features of texts or intermediate values within LLMs, watermarking techniques that introduce ``green tokens'' or embed ``secret keys'' as vectors to predicted outputs, and prompt-based approaches that make use of (another) LLM as detectors. A comprehensive overview of these approaches can be found in \citet{wu2023survey}.

While the relevant recent studies on the detection task primarily adopt surface-level features, such as distributions of n-grams and part-of-speech (POS) tags, we approach the problem from another angle, namely using hierarchical discourse features within texts. This direction is reminiscent of the work by \citet{corston-oliver-etal-2001-machine}, who, in the pre-LLM era, explored a comparable path. Their study delved into the utility of branching properties of POS trees to ascertain whether texts were machine-generated, specifically by a machine translation model. In our study, we consider discourse-level features that span over multiple sentences and paragraphs (as opposed to sentence-level POS features).

The existing challenges in the field encompass several facets. First, there are pronounced out-of-distribution (OOD) challenges, wherein the detectors struggle when confronted with texts that fall outside the learned distribution. Second, the detectors are prone to potential attacks, such as paraphrasing attacks, where machine-generated texts undergo further paraphrasing to alter the distribution of lexical and syntactic features. This dynamic renders detectors reliant on surface-level features and watermarking technology ineffective. Last but not least, the inherent ambiguities present in the two groups of texts are getting more nuanced, making it progressively challenging to distinguish them. In this paper, we touch upon these three challenges by showing that the addition of discourse features (1) improves detecting OOD samples, and (2) trains a more robust classifier against paraphrased attacks. Furthermore, our analyses, utilizing discourse network motifs, shed light on the nuanced distinctions within the hierarchical structures of the two text categories.

  abstract = 	 {Potential harms of large language models can be mitigated by watermarking model output, i.e., embedding signals into generated text that are invisible to humans but algorithmically detectable from a short span of tokens. before_text:
\section{Related Work}\label{sec:related_work}
With the advent of recent LLMs, the need as well as the challenges in distinguishing machine-generated texts has risen significantly. Various existing methodologies encompass finetuning LMs \citep{guo2023close,liu2023argugpt,liu2023check,li2023deepfake} with supervised datasets, potentially utilizing techniques like contrastive learning \citep{liu2023coco,bhattacharjee2023conda}, adversarial learning \citep{shi2023red,yang2023chatgpt}, or human-assisted learning \citep{uchendu2023does,dugan-etal-2023-roft}. Additionally, there are zero-shot methods \citep{Beresneva2016ComputerGeneratedTD,vasilatos2023howkgpt} that leverage statistical features of texts or intermediate values within LLMs, watermarking techniques \that introduce ``green tokens'' or embed ``secret keys'' as vectors to predicted outputs, and prompt-based approaches that make use of (another) LLM as detectors. A comprehensive overview of these approaches can be found in \citet{wu2023survey}.

While the relevant recent studies on the detection task primarily adopt surface-level features, such as distributions of n-grams and part-of-speech (POS) tags, we approach the problem from another angle, namely using hierarchical discourse features within texts. This direction is reminiscent of the work by \citet{corston-oliver-etal-2001-machine}, who, in the pre-LLM era, explored a comparable path. Their study delved into the utility of branching properties of POS trees to ascertain whether texts were machine-generated, specifically by a machine translation model. In our study, we consider discourse-level features that span over multiple sentences and paragraphs (as opposed to sentence-level POS features).

The existing challenges in the field encompass several facets. First, there are pronounced out-of-distribution (OOD) challenges, wherein the detectors struggle when confronted with texts that fall outside the learned distribution. Second, the detectors are prone to potential attacks, such as paraphrasing attacks, where machine-generated texts undergo further paraphrasing to alter the distribution of lexical and syntactic features. This dynamic renders detectors reliant on surface-level features and watermarking technology ineffective. Last but not least, the inherent ambiguities present in the two groups of texts are getting more nuanced, making it progressively challenging to distinguish them. In this paper, we touch upon these three challenges by showing that the addition of discourse features (1) improves detecting OOD samples, and (2) trains a more robust classifier against paraphrased attacks. Furthermore, our analyses, utilizing discourse network motifs, shed light on the nuanced distinctions within the hierarchical structures of the two text categories.

before_text:
\section{Limitations}\label{sec:limitation}
In our study, the validity of our experiments and subsequent findings relies on the parsed discourse trees generated by an existing parser. It is important to acknowledge the potential for using alternative discourse frameworks like Segmented Discourse Representation Theory \citep{lascarides2007segmented} though finding or training a robust parser is a challenge.
Despite employing a state-of-the-art model for RST parsing, it is crucial to recognize that parsed results may still be suboptimal due to the inherent difficulty of hierarchical discourse parsing. This challenge is exacerbated by the limited scale of existing datasets used for model training and the inherent ambiguities present in discourse relations. In this aspect, an important future direction would be to build a more robust discourse parser using current LLMs.

In our experimental setup, motif analyses were carried out using motif sizes of 3, 6, and 9. It is worth considering that longer and potentially significant patterns may exist beyond these sizes. However, expanding the motif sizes within our current computational approach is not feasible. Nevertheless, there is potential to capture the distributions of longer patterns through approximation techniques, such as subsampling or leveraging deep learning methodologies.

In the experiment investigating the correlation between formality scores and the number of hyperedges (Section \ref{subsubsec:formality_scorer}), it is important to acknowledge a limitation: the formality scorer had been trained on short sentence-level inputs and may not be adequately suited for assessing the formality of longer documents spanning multiple paragraphs.

Furthermore, our study highlights the need for more comprehensive analysis within the \textsc{TenPageStories} dataset. Exploring the correlation between discourse structures and specific linguistic features tailored to creative writing, such as tagged events between named entities, presents an intriguing avenue for future research.

@article{milo2002networkmotifs,
    author = {R. Milo  and S. Shen-Orr  and S. Itzkovitz  and N. Kashtan  and D. Chklovskii  and U. Alon },
    title = {Network Motifs: Simple Building Blocks of Complex Networks},
    journal = {Science},
    volume = {298},
    number = {5594},
    pages = {824-827},
    year = {2002},
    doi = {10.1126/science.298.5594.824},
    URL = {https://www.science.org/doi/abs/10.1126/science.298.5594.824},
    eprint = {https://www.science.org/doi/pdf/10.1126/science.298.5594.824},
    abstract = {Complex networks are studied across many fields of science. To uncover their structural design principles, we defined “network motifs,” patterns of interconnections occurring in complex networks at numbers that are significantly higher than those in randomized networks. We found such motifs in networks from biochemistry, neurobiology, ecology, and engineering. The motifs shared by ecological food webs were distinct from the motifs shared by the genetic networks of Escherichia coli and Saccharomyces cerevisiae or from those found in the World Wide Web. Similar motifs were found in networks that perform information processing, even though they describe elements as different as biomolecules within a cell and synaptic connections between neurons in Caenorhabditis elegans. Motifs may thus define universal classes of networks. This approach may uncover the basic building blocks of most networks.}
}

@inproceedings{10.5555/3524938.3525989,
author = {Zhang, Jingqing and Zhao, Yao and Saleh, Mohammad and Liu, Peter J.},
title = {PEGASUS: pre-training with extracted gap-sentences for abstractive summarization},
year = {2020},
publisher = {JMLR.org},
abstract = {Recent work pre-training Transformers with self-supervised objectives on large text corpora has shown great success when fine-tuned on downstream NLP tasks including text summarization. However, pre-training objectives tailored for abstractive text summarization have not been explored. Furthermore there is a lack of systematic evaluation across diverse domains. In this work, we propose pretraining large Transformer-based encoder-decoder models on massive text corpora with a new self-supervised objective. In PEGASUS, important sentences are removed/masked from an input document and are generated together as one output sequence from the remaining sentences, similar to an extractive summary. We evaluated our best PEGASUS model on 12 downstream summarization tasks spanning news, science, stories, instructions, emails, patents, and legislative bills. Experiments demonstrate it achieves state-of-the-art performance on all 12 downstream datasets measured by ROUGE scores. Our model also shows surprising performance on low-resource summarization, surpassing previous state-of-the-art results on 6 datasets with only 1000 examples. Finally we validated our results using human evaluation and show that our model summaries achieve human performance on multiple datasets.},
booktitle = {Proceedings of the 37th International Conference on Machine Learning},
articleno = {1051},
numpages = {12},
series = {ICML'20}
}

before_text:
\section{Limitations}\label{sec:limitation}
In our study, the validity of our experiments and subsequent findings relies on the parsed discourse trees generated by an existing parser. It is important to acknowledge the potential for using alternative discourse frameworks like Segmented Discourse Representation Theory \citep{lascarides2007segmented} though finding or training a robust parser is a challenge.
Despite employing a state-of-the-art model for RST parsing, it is crucial to recognize that parsed results may still be suboptimal due to the inherent difficulty of hierarchical discourse parsing. This challenge is exacerbated by the limited scale of existing datasets used for model training and the inherent ambiguities present in discourse relations. In this aspect, an important future direction would be to build a more robust discourse parser using current LLMs.

In our experimental setup, motif analyses were carried out using motif sizes of 3, 6, and 9. It is worth considering that longer and potentially significant patterns may exist beyond these sizes. However, expanding the motif sizes within our current computational approach is not feasible. Nevertheless, there is potential to capture the distributions of longer patterns through approximation techniques, such as subsampling or leveraging deep learning methodologies.

In the experiment investigating the correlation between formality scores and the number of hyperedges (Section \ref{subsubsec:formality_scorer}), it is important to acknowledge a limitation: the formality scorer had been trained on short sentence-level inputs and may not be adequately suited for assessing the formality of longer documents spanning multiple paragraphs.

Furthermore, our study highlights the need for more comprehensive analysis within the \textsc{TenPageStories} dataset. Exploring the correlation between discourse structures and specific linguistic features tailored to creative writing, such as tagged events between named entities, presents an intriguing avenue for future research.

From Table \ref{tab:overall_f1}, we can see that incorporating motif information consistently enhances classification performance across various base encoders, underscoring the effectiveness of discourse motifs as auxiliary information for capturing deeper linguistic structures beyond surface lexicons. Specifically, when the GAT model leverages the overall hierarchical discourse structure, notable performance gains are observed (e.g., 0.67 $\rightarrow$ 0.73 on \textsc{HC3}). Additionally, given the LF model's proficiency in comprehending lengthy texts, the inclusion of discourse motifs offers explicit structural insights, further enhancing the performance. Even on the paraphrased out-of-domain test set (``OOD-Para''), where samples come from ``unseen domains'' and ``unseen models,'' augmenting the detector with discourse motifs yields significant improvements over the baselines. A subsequent experiment (\S\ref{subsec:upper_vs_lower}) indicates that this enhancement stems from discourse-level information being \textit{less susceptible} to lexical variations induced by sentence-level paraphrasing attacks.

after_text:
From Table \ref{tab:overall_f1}, we can see that incorporating motif information consistently enhances classification performance across various base encoders, underscoring the effectiveness of discourse motifs as auxiliary information for capturing deeper linguistic structures beyond surface lexicons. Specifically, when the GAT model leverages the overall hierarchical discourse structure, notable performance gains are observed (e.g., 0.67 $\rightarrow$ 0.73 on \textsc{HC3}). Additionally, given the LF model's proficiency in comprehending lengthy texts, the inclusion of discourse motifs offers explicit structural insights, further enhancing the performance. Even on the paraphrased out-of-domain test set (``OOD-Para''), where samples come from ``unseen domains'' and ``unseen models,'' augmenting the detector with discourse motifs yields significant improvements over the baselines. A subsequent experiment (\S\ref{subsec:upper_vs_lower}) indicates that this enhancement stems from discourse-level information being \textit{less susceptible} to lexical variations induced by sentence-level paraphrasing attacks.
